preamble.summary: The most beloved psalm in the Psalter — six verses of absolute trust. The speaker declares the LORD as shepherd, provider, guide, protector, and host. The imagery moves from open pasture (vv. 1-3) through a dark valley (v. 4) to a banquet table prepared in the presence of enemies (v. 5), and closes with the assurance of faithful love pursuing the speaker all the days of his life. The psalm contains no petition, no complaint, no confession — only confidence.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 23 is positioned immediately after Psalm 22, the most anguished lament in the Psalter. This placement is not accidental. The sufferer who passed through the valley of the shadow of death in Psalm 22 — mocked, pierced, surrounded by enemies, laid in the dust of death — now emerges on the other side and can say 'The LORD is my shepherd; I lack nothing.' The green pastures and still waters are where you arrive after the cross. The 'table prepared in the presence of enemies' (v. 5) does not mean the enemies are gone — they are still watching. It means the shepherd feeds his own even while the wolves circle. The shift from third person ('he leads me') to second person ('you are with me') happens exactly at the darkest point — verse 4, the valley of the shadow of death. When the danger is greatest, the distance between speaker and God collapses from 'he' to 'you.'
preamble.friction: The psalm's two controlling metaphors — shepherd (vv. 1-4) and host (vv. 5-6) — are not as separate as they first appear. In the ancient Near East, kings were called shepherds of their people, and hospitality was a royal obligation. Both images describe a single relationship: the LORD as the one who provides, protects, and honors. The phrase gei tsalmavet (v. 4) is traditionally rendered 'valley of the shadow of death,' but tsalmavet may be a compound of tsel ('shadow') and mavet ('death'), or it may be an intensified form of tselem ('deep shadow, darkness'). Both readings yield approximately the same meaning: the darkest, most dangerous passage imaginable. We retain 'shadow of death' because the traditional rendering accurately captures the semantic weight.
preamble.connections: The shepherd metaphor for God appears throughout the Hebrew Bible: Genesis 48:15 (Jacob's blessing), Genesis 49:24 ('the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel'), Psalm 80:1 ('Shepherd of Israel'), Isaiah 40:11 ('he will feed his flock like a shepherd'), Ezekiel 34 (God as the true shepherd replacing failed human shepherds). Jesus claims the title in John 10:11: 'I am the good shepherd.' The anointing with oil (v. 5) connects to the anointing of priests (Exodus 29:7) and kings (1 Samuel 16:13). The 'house of the LORD' in verse 6 anticipates the temple theology of Psalms 27:4 and 84:1-4. The overflowing cup (v. 5) reappears in Psalm 116:13 as 'the cup of salvation.'

verses.text_hebrew: מִזְמ֥וֹר לְדָוִ֑ד יְהוָ֥ה רֹ֝עִ֗י לֹ֣א אֶחְסָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: A Psalm of David. The LORD is my shepherd; I shall not want.
verses.rendering:
A psalm of David.
The LORD is my shepherd —
I lack nothing.
verses.expanded_rendering: The superscription and the opening declaration arrive as a single breath. Mizmor le-David ('a psalm of David') followed immediately by YHWH ro'i ('the LORD is my shepherd'). The metaphor is intimate and specific: ro'eh ('shepherd') is not a king surveying subjects from a distance but a working shepherd who walks with the flock, sleeps with the flock, fights off predators for the flock. The consequence — lo echsar ('I do not lack, I will not lack') — is total. The verb chasar means 'to lack, to be without, to be deficient.' With the LORD as shepherd, the speaker has no deficit of any kind. This is not prosperity theology; it is the confidence of a sheep who knows its shepherd personally.
translator_notes: In WLC versification, the superscription and first poetic line share verse 1. The possessive suffix on ro'i ('my shepherd') makes this a personal claim, not a theological abstraction. The speaker does not say 'The LORD is a shepherd' or 'The LORD shepherds Israel' — he says 'The LORD is my shepherd.' The intimacy is the point.

key_terms.semantic_range: shepherd, one who tends flocks, feeder, companion, pastor
key_terms.note: Ro'eh comes from the root ra'ah ('to pasture, tend, graze, feed'). A shepherd in the ancient Near East was simultaneously provider (finding pasture and water), protector (fighting predators), healer (binding wounds), and guide (knowing the terrain). The title was applied to kings throughout Mesopotamia and Egypt. When David — himself a former shepherd — calls God his shepherd, every dimension of care is implied.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּנְא֣וֹת דֶּ֭שֶׁא יַרְבִּיצֵ֑נִי עַל־מֵ֖י מְנֻח֣וֹת יְנַהֲלֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: He maketh me to lie down in green pastures: he leadeth me beside the still waters.
verses.rendering:
In green pastures he makes me lie down;
beside still waters he leads me.
verses.expanded_rendering: Two images of provision and peace. Neot deshe ('pastures of tender grass') describes lush grazing land — not scrubby desert brush but rich, green growth. The verb yarbitseni ('he makes me lie down') is significant: sheep lie down only when they feel safe, fed, and free from disturbance. A shepherd cannot force sheep to lie down; he can only create the conditions in which they choose to rest. The second image — mei menuchot ('waters of rest, still waters') — describes calm, accessible water. Sheep will not drink from rushing streams; they fear fast-moving water. The shepherd leads (yenahaleni) them to water that is quiet and approachable. Both images describe a God who accommodates himself to the vulnerability of his flock.
translator_notes: The verb nahal ('to lead, guide') specifically describes leading to water — it is used of leading flocks to drinking places (Genesis 33:14, Isaiah 49:10). The shepherd does not merely point the way; he walks the path and brings the sheep to what they need.

key_terms.semantic_range: rest, resting place, quiet, repose, settled condition
key_terms.note: Menuchah means 'rest, resting place, settled peace.' It is the word used for the Promised Land as Israel's place of rest (Deuteronomy 12:9, Psalm 95:11) and for the resting place of the ark (Numbers 10:33). The still waters are not merely calm — they are the waters of divine rest, the refreshment that comes from God's own settled peace.

verses.text_hebrew: נַפְשִׁ֣י יְשׁוֹבֵ֑ב יַנְחֵ֥נִי בְמַעְגְּלֵי־צֶ֝֗דֶק לְמַ֣עַן שְׁמֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He restoreth my soul: he leadeth me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake.
verses.expanded_rendering: Nafshi yeshovev ('he restores my soul/life') uses the verb shuv ('to return, restore, bring back'). The shepherd brings back the wandering sheep, or he restores the exhausted sheep's vitality — both meanings operate simultaneously. The magelei-tsedeq ('tracks of righteousness, right paths') are literally the wagon-ruts or well-worn trails that lead to good pasture. Tsedeq here means 'right, correct, proper' — the paths that achieve their purpose, that lead where they should lead. And the reason for all of this — leman shemo ('for the sake of his name') — relocates the motive from the sheep's merit to the shepherd's reputation. God leads in right paths not because the sheep deserves it but because God's own name and character are at stake. A shepherd is judged by the condition of his flock.
translator_notes: Leman shemo ('for the sake of his name') is a critical theological qualifier. The shepherd's care is grounded in the shepherd's own character, not the worthiness of the sheep. This removes all performance anxiety from the relationship — the sheep is cared for because of who the shepherd is.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּ֤ם כִּֽי־אֵלֵ֨ךְ בְּגֵ֪יא צַלְמָ֡וֶת לֹא־אִ֘ירָ֤א רָ֗ע כִּי־אַתָּ֥ה עִמָּדִ֑י שִׁבְטְךָ֥ וּ֝מִשְׁעַנְתֶּ֗ךָ הֵ֣מָּה יְנַחֲמֻֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil: for thou art with me; thy rod and thy staff they comfort me.
verses.rendering:
Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death,
I will fear no evil,
for you are with me.
Your rod and your staff — they comfort me.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm descends into darkness. Gei tsalmavet ('the valley of the shadow of death' or 'the valley of deep darkness') is the most dangerous terrain in the shepherd's world — a narrow ravine where predators lurk and footing is treacherous. The speaker does not deny the valley's existence or pretend it is not dark. He walks through it (elekh — ongoing, continuous walking). But the fear that should accompany such a journey is absent: lo ira ra ('I will not fear evil/harm'). The reason is not courage or denial but presence: ki attah immadi ('for you are with me'). This is the moment the psalm shifts from 'he' to 'you' — from describing God in the third person to addressing God directly. The intimacy increases precisely when the danger increases. The rod (shevet) is for fighting off predators; the staff (mishenet) is for guiding and supporting the sheep. Together they represent both the shepherd's violence against threats and his gentleness toward the flock. Hemah yenachamuni ('they comfort me') — the word nacham means 'to comfort, console, give relief.' The weapons and tools of the shepherd are themselves a source of comfort, because they prove the shepherd is present and prepared.
translator_notes: The grammatical shift from third person to second person at ki attah immadi ('for you are with me') is one of the most noted features of the psalm. When speaking of green pastures and still waters — places of safety — the speaker can describe God at a distance ('he'). When death closes in, distance collapses and the speaker speaks directly to God ('you'). Theology becomes prayer at the point of greatest need.

Tsalmavet may be a compound noun (tsel + mavet = 'shadow of death') or an intensive form meaning 'deep darkness.' The traditional 'shadow of death' reading, supported by the Septuagint (skia thanatou), captures the full weight of the image and has been retained.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּעֲרֹ֬ךְ לְפָנַ֨י ׀ שֻׁלְחָ֗ן נֶ֥גֶד צֹרְרָ֑י דִּשַּׁ֖נְתָּ בַשֶּׁ֥מֶן רֹ֝אשִׁ֗י כּוֹסִ֥י רְוָיָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou preparest a table before me in the presence of mine enemies: thou anointest my head with oil; my cup runneth over.
verses.rendering:
You prepare a table before me
in the presence of my enemies.
You anoint my head with oil;
my cup overflows.
verses.expanded_rendering: The metaphor shifts from shepherd to host, but the relationship remains the same: the LORD provides, protects, and honors. The table (shulchan) is set neged tsorerai ('in front of my enemies') — the enemies are not removed; they are forced to watch while God lavishes care on the one they intended to destroy. This is not private comfort but public vindication. The anointing with oil (dishanta va-shemen roshi) is the gesture of an honored guest — perfumed oil poured on the head signals welcome, celebration, and special status (Luke 7:46). The overflowing cup (kosi revayah) means the provision exceeds the capacity to contain it. Revayah means 'saturation, abundance, overflow.' The speaker receives more than he can hold.
translator_notes: The transition from shepherd imagery to banquet imagery is not a break in the psalm but a deepening. In the ancient Near East, a king's hospitality was an extension of his shepherding role. To set a table for someone in the presence of their enemies was a political and social statement: this person is under my protection, and anyone who touches them answers to me.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֤ךְ ׀ ט֤וֹב וָחֶ֣סֶד יִ֭רְדְּפוּנִי כׇּל־יְמֵ֣י חַיָּ֑י וְשַׁבְתִּ֥י בְּבֵית־יְ֝הוָ֗ה לְאֹ֣רֶךְ יָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life: and I will dwell in the house of the LORD for ever.
verses.rendering:
Surely goodness and faithful love will pursue me
all the days of my life,
and I will dwell in the house of the LORD
for length of days.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse contains its most surprising verb: yirdefuni ('they will pursue me, chase me, hunt me'). The word radaf is the verb used for military pursuit, for predators chasing prey, for enemies hounding fugitives. But here, what pursues the speaker is not danger but tov va-chesed ('goodness and faithful love'). God's goodness hunts the speaker down. You cannot escape divine kindness. The faithful love (chesed) that defines God's covenant character is not passive — it actively chases, tracks, and overtakes. The final destination — beit-YHWH ('the house of the LORD') — is where the pursued one finally comes to rest. Le-orekh yamim ('for length of days') means 'for a very long time' or 'as long as days last.' The speaker's life will be spent in God's presence, not as imprisonment but as homecoming. The sheep has been led from green pastures through the valley of death to the house of the LORD, and there it stays.
translator_notes: The verb radaf ('pursue, chase') is deliberately chosen to invert the language of threat. Throughout the psalms, enemies pursue (rodeph) the righteous (Psalm 7:2, 31:15, 35:3). Here, it is goodness and chesed that pursue. The hunted becomes the beloved.

Le-orekh yamim ('for length of days') is the same phrase used in Psalm 21:5 for the king's extended life. It suggests not eternity in the abstract but the fullness of lived time — all the days, every day, without interruption.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant devotion, mercy, steadfast love
key_terms.note: Chesed here is not quiet, stationary mercy waiting to be found. It is aggressive, pursuing, relentless love. The verb yirdefuni ('will pursue me') transforms chesed from a passive attribute into an active force that chases the speaker through every circumstance. Chesed will not let him go.
